title: Trauma and the Body's Boundaries: Reclaiming Your Physical and Emotional Space
excerpt: Understand how trauma disrupts your body's boundaries and learn science-backed ways to reclaim your physical and emotional space.
body:
Introduction: Trauma and the Invisible Lines of Our Bodies

Trauma doesn’t just live in the mind; it imprints itself deeply into our bodies and distorts our sense of physical and emotional boundaries. When your boundaries feel porous, violated, or inconsistent, it’s often the aftershock of trauma signaling through your nervous system. Understanding and reclaiming these boundaries is crucial for anyone stuck in cycles of overwhelm, dissociation, or emotional flooding despite therapy.

This article explores how trauma affects bodily boundaries, why this matters for healing, and practical, somatic-based tools grounded in cutting-edge research by pioneers like Bessel van der Kolk, Peter Levine, and Stephen Porges.

What Are Boundaries, Really? Beyond the Social Definitions

Boundaries often get talked about as social or psychological limits: saying no, enforcing personal space, or protecting yourself emotionally. But boundaries are first and foremost embodied experiences. They’re the felt sense of where “you” end and “not you” begins.

Trauma fractures this felt sense, making the distinction between self and other less clear. You might experience this as:

  • Feeling like your body isn’t your own
  • Being hyperaware or hypersensitive to others’ emotions or physical proximity
  • Difficulty staying grounded in your body
  • Shifting between feeling numb and overwhelmed

These symptoms point to a nervous system struggling to regulate the boundary between safe inside and dangerous outside.

Trauma’s Impact on the Nervous System and Boundaries

According to Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory, our nervous system constantly scans for safety and danger to regulate social engagement and defense mechanisms. Trauma can hijack this system, making your body feel unsafe even in neutral environments.
Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, describes trauma as a freeze in the nervous system’s natural defensive responses. This freeze disrupts your internal map of physical space and bodily integrity.

Bessel van der Kolk highlights in The Body Keeps the Score how trauma is stored not just in memories, but in the body’s sensory and motor systems. This storage affects how boundaries are perceived and maintained:

“Traumatized people lose the ability to be in the present, which is the only place where they can feel safe.” – Bessel van der Kolk

When you can’t reliably distinguish your body from the environment or others, boundaries become blurred or rigid, leading to either withdrawal or hypervigilance.

Somatic Markers of Disturbed Bodily Boundaries

Pat Ogden, a somatic trauma expert, identifies several body-based signs of boundary disruption:

  • Proprioceptive confusion: Losing clear awareness of where your body parts are in space.
  • Interoceptive numbness: Difficulty sensing internal bodily states like hunger or tension.
  • Hyperarousal or hypoarousal: Feeling either constantly on edge or shut down.
  • Dissociation: Feeling disconnected from your body or emotions.

These markers show how trauma compromises your embodied self-awareness and boundary integrity.

Reclaiming Boundaries Through Somatic Practices

Healing requires rebuilding that felt sense of “me” versus “not me.” Here are evidence-based somatic tools:

1. Grounding and Orienting

Deb Dana, a leading Polyvagal-informed therapist, emphasizes the importance of orienting your senses to the present environment to restore safety. Simple practices include:

  • Noticing sounds, smells, and sights around you
  • Feeling your feet on the floor or your back against a chair
  • Slow, mindful breathing to downregulate hyperarousal

This helps recalibrate your nervous system’s safety cues and reinforces your physical boundaries.

2. Proprioceptive Awareness

Peter Levine’s Somatic Experiencing uses gentle movement to enhance proprioception—the sense of body position. Exercises such as slow stretching, gentle rocking, or mindful walking teach your nervous system how to feel safe and stable within your skin.

3. Safe Touch and Containment

Safe, consensual touch—like self-hugs or weighted blankets—can help rebuild a sense of containment. The pressure input informs the body that it is held and protected, which is crucial when trauma has caused boundary confusion.

4. Resourcing and Parts Work

Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems (IFS) model pairs well with somatic work. Identifying and dialoguing with parts of yourself that hold fear or boundary issues can clarify internal emotional and physical limits.

The Role of Emotional Boundaries in Trauma Recovery

Physical boundary work and emotional boundary work are inseparable. Gabor Maté emphasizes that trauma often leads to emotional enmeshment or dissociation—either feeling overwhelmed by others’ emotions or shut down to your own.

Dan Siegel’s concept of the "window of tolerance" shows that emotional regulation depends on staying within a zone where you can feel and respond without flooding or shutting down. Reclaiming boundaries means expanding this window through somatic regulation.

Practical Steps to Start Reclaiming Your Boundaries Today

  • Start small: Notice how your body feels in different spaces and with different people.
  • Practice grounding: Use sensory anchoring like feeling your feet or hands.
  • Set micro-boundaries: Use your breath or subtle movements to create a felt sense of space around you.
  • Seek out safe touch: Use self-holding or weighted blankets if appropriate.
  • Journal sensations and feelings: Track your boundary experiences to increase awareness.

When to Seek Professional Support

Some boundary disruptions can feel overwhelming or retraumatizing to address alone. Working with trauma-informed somatic therapists trained in SE, IFS, or Polyvagal-informed therapy can provide safety and structure.

Francine Shapiro’s EMDR therapy is also effective in processing trauma stored in the body, helping to integrate traumatic memories and reduce boundary confusion.

Conclusion: Your Body Remembers and Can Heal

Trauma fractures your body’s physical and emotional boundaries, leaving you feeling unsafe, disconnected, or overwhelmed. But your nervous system is plastic—it can learn new ways of recognizing and maintaining healthy boundaries.

Grounding, proprioceptive awareness, safe touch, and internal parts work are evidence-based paths to reclaiming your physical and emotional space. The journey isn’t easy, but as Bessel van der Kolk reminds us, reconnecting with your body is the foundation for lasting trauma recovery.

Reclaim your boundaries. Reclaim yourself.

faqs.q: How does trauma affect my sense of personal space?
faqs.a: Trauma disrupts your nervous system’s ability to regulate safety, making your sense of physical and emotional boundaries feel unclear or violated. This can cause hypervigilance or dissociation.

faqs.q: What are somatic practices for boundary healing?
faqs.a: Somatic practices include grounding, proprioceptive exercises, safe touch, and parts work. These help your nervous system relearn safety and rebuild your felt sense of bodily boundaries.

faqs.q: Can therapy alone fix boundary issues from trauma?
faqs.a: Talk therapy often isn’t enough because trauma is stored in the body. Integrating somatic-based approaches addresses the nervous system directly, which is crucial for restoring boundaries.

faqs.q: When should I seek professional help for boundary issues?
faqs.a: If boundary disruptions cause intense distress, dissociation, or retraumatization, working with trauma-informed somatic therapists or EMDR practitioners can provide needed support and safety.
